Preservation of mitochondrial functional integrity in mitochondria
isolated from small cryopreserved mouse brain areas
Daniela Valentia,⇑, Lidia de Baria, Bianca De Filippisb, Laura Ricceric, Rosa Anna Vaccaa,⇑
aInstitute of Biomembranes and Bioenergetics, National Council of Research, 70126 Bari, Italy
bSection of Behavioural Neuroscience, Department of Cell Biology and Neurosciences, Istituto Superiore di Sanità, 00161 Roma, Italy

Keywords:
Brain mitochondria
CryopreservationMitochondria isolationMitochondrial oxidative phosphorylationabstract
Studies of mitochondrial bioenergetics in brain pathophysiology are often precluded by the need to iso-
late mitochondria immediately after tissue dissection from a large number of brain biopsies for compar-
ative studies Here we present a procedure of cryopreservation of small brain areas from which
mitochondrial enriched fractions (crude mitochondria) with high oxidative phosphorylation efﬁciencycan be isolated Small mouse brain areas were frozen and stored in a solution containing glycerol as cryo-
protectant Crude mitochondria were isolated by differential centrifugation from both cryopreserved and
freshly explanted brain samples and were compared with respect to their ability to generate membranepotential and produce ATP Intactness of outer and inner mitochondrial membranes was veriﬁed bypolarographic ascorbate and cytochrome ctests and spectrophotometric assay of citrate synthase activ-
ity Preservation of structural integrity and oxidative phosphorylation efﬁciency was successfully
obtained in crude mitochondria isolated from different areas of cryopreserved mouse brain samples.Long-term cryopreservation of small brain areas from which intact and phosphorylating mitochondria
can be isolated for the study of mitochondrial bioenergetics will signiﬁcantly expand the study of mito-
chondrial defects in neurological pathologies, allowing large comparative studies and favoring interlab-oratory and interdisciplinary analyses /C2112013 Elsevier Inc All rights reserved Mitochondrial energy metabolism is crucial for providing the
cell with the fuel needed to perform a host of biological processes;
likewise, dysregulation of mitochondrial energy metabolism is di-
rectly involved in the pathogenic mechanisms of a plethora of
pathological states [1,2]  Mitochondrial defects are usually analyzed in frozen biopsy
samples using histochemical, enzymatic, or molecular biology
methods Although the maximal activities of key mitochondrial en-
zymes, such as citrate synthase and cytochrome coxidase (COX),1
have been widely used as indicative of mitochondrial oxidative
capacity [3–5] , it is unlikely that measurement of individual enzyme
activities can accurately reﬂect the overall function of an organelle as
complex as the mitochondrion Thus, a critical requirement is toimplement more direct analyses of mitochondrial function by mea-
suring their ability to synthesize ATP via oxidative phosphorylation,which provides more complete information and, in addition, repre-
sents a relevant screening procedure to check potential mitochon-
drial bioenergetic dysfunctions Studies on mitochondrial function are conventionally per-
formed in mitochondria isolated from fresh tissues or cultured
cells because they yield tightly coupled mitochondria; on the con-
trary, mitochondria from frozen tissues are inevitably poorly intact
and coupled Thus, the major limitation of investigating mitochon-
drial function is that it must be performed immediately after tissue
explants, with this being not always feasible due to both time and
technical constraints This limitation reduces the possibility to per-
form basic mitochondrial studies on brain bioenergetics and to
analyze mitochondrial defects in various neurological diseases Therefore, there is a compelling need to obtain functional mito-
chondria from long-term preserved tissues Moreover, because a
limited number of centers perform functional analysis of mito-
chondria, tissue cryopreservation is also crucial to ship samples
to distant laboratories for additional assays Previous studies demonstrated preservation of mitochondria in-
side cryopreserved sperm, cardiac, and skeletal muscle ﬁbers [6–8]
as well as cryopreservation of isolated brain mitochondria [9] 0003-2697/$ - see front matter /C2112013 Elsevier Inc All rights reserved http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2013.08.030⇑Corresponding authors Fax: +39 80 5443317 E-mail addresses: d.valenti@ibbe.cnr.it (D Valenti), r.vacca@ibbe.cnr.it (R.A Vacca) 1Abbreviations used: COX, cytochrome coxidase; ROT, rotenone; AA, antimycin A;

Here we demonstrate, for the ﬁrst time, that mouse brain mito-
chondria can be isolated from cryopreserved frozen small brain
biopsies, maintaining their intactness and an optimal efﬁciency
of oxidative phosphorylation process comparable to that of mito-
chondria isolated from fresh mouse brain tissues Materials and methods
Chemicals
All reagents and enzymes were obtained from Sigma (St Louis,
MO, USA) with the exception of Triton X-100, Hepes, and Tris (Ba-
ker)

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Cryopreservation of small brain areas from which mitochondrial enriched fractions can be isolated.
2. Small mouse brain areas were frozen and stored in a solution containing glycerol as cryoprotectant.
3. Crude mitochondria were isolated by differential centrifugation from both cryopreserved and freshly explanted brain samples.
4. Intactness of outer and inner mitochondrial membranes was verified by polarographic ascorbate and cytochrome c tests and spectrophotometric assay of citrate synthase activity.
5. Preservation of structural integrity and oxidative phosphorylation efficiency was successfully obtained in crude mitochondria isolated from different areas of cryopreserved mouse brain samples.

**Conclusion:** The article describes a procedure for cryopreservation of small brain areas and subsequent isolation of crude mitochondria with preserved structural integrity and oxidative phosphorylation efficiency. The steps mentioned in the article are complete and provide a clear protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from cryopreserved brain samples.

summary: Researchers at the National Council of Research and the Institute Superiore di Sanità, including Daniela Valentia, Lidia de Baria, Bianca De Filippis, Laura Ricceri, and Rosa Anna Vaccaa, have developed a cryopreservation technique for small brain areas that allows for the isolation of intact and phosphorylating mitochondria. The cryopreserved brain samples were compared to freshly explanted samples, and the mitochondria from both were found to have preserved structural integrity and oxidative phosphorylation efficiency. This technique overcomes the limitation of investigating mitochondrial function immediately after tissue explants and enables basic mitochondrial studies on brain bioenergetics and the analysis of mitochondrial defects in neurological diseases. The preservation of mitochondria in cryopreserved tissues has also been demonstrated in sperm, cardiac muscle, and skeletal muscle. This study significantly expands the possibilities for studying mitochondrial defects in neurological pathologies and facilitates interlaboratory and interdisciplinary analyses. The researchers successfully preserved mitochondrial functional integrity in mitochondria isolated from cryopreserved mouse brain areas, which will greatly enhance the study of mitochondrial bioenergetics in neurological pathophysiology.

(ROT), antimycin A (AA), mixothiazol, oligomycin, and carbonyl

solved in ethanol Animal experimental model and brain tissue dissection
Female mice of an outbred CD-1 Swiss-derived strain (ICR) were
purchased from a commercial breeder (Harlan, Italy) On arrival,
the animals were housed in an air-conditioned room (tempera-
ture = 21 ± 1 /C176C, relative humidity = 60 ± 10%, lights on from 20.00
to 08.00 h) Animals were housed in groups of 2 or 3 individuals
in polycarbonate transparent cages (33 /C213/C214 cm) with a metal
top and sawdust bedding and were provided with a complete pel-
let diet ad libitum (Altromin, Germany) and tap water At approximately 4 months of age, animals were sacriﬁced and
their brains were dissected Either entire (right or left) hemi-
spheres or isolated brain regions (cortex, hippocampus, striatum,
and cerebellum) were carefully dissected Brain samples were
immediately processed for mitochondria isolation (fresh samples),
frozen in dry ice (frozen samples), or cryopreserved as described
below Cryopreservation of different regions of brain samples
For cryopreservation of brain tissue, dissections were carried
out in ice-cold solution consisting of 50 mM K-Mes (pH 7.1),
3m MK 2HPO 4, 9.5 mM MgCl 2, and 3 mM ATP Brain samples were
then transferred into a cryotube containing 500 ll of the same
solution plus 20% glycerol and 10 mg/ml fatty acid-free bovine ser-
um albumin (BSA) Samples were then frozen in liquid isopentaneto achieve a rapid and uniform freezing and were maintained at
/C030 to /C035/C176C Subsequently all samples were stored at /C080/C176C
or stored in dry ice and shipped For mitochondria isolation, tubes containing cryopreserved tis-
sues were placed on ice, and when the cryopreservation solution
was almost completely thawed, brain tissues were immediately
transferred and washed in ice-cold respiratory medium consisting
of 210 mM mannitol, 70 mM sucrose, 3 mM MgCl 2, 20 mM Tris–
HCl, and 5 mM KH 2PO4/K2HPO 4(pH 7.4) plus 2 mg/ml BSA Mito-
chondria were then isolated as described below Mitochondria preparation
Mitochondria were isolated from fresh, frozen, or cryopreserved
mouse brain samples, essentially as described in Ref [10] with
some modiﬁcations All procedures were carried out at 4 /C176C Brieﬂy,
brain samples were homogenized (5 strokes at 500 rpm) in sucrose
medium consisting of 0.25 M sucrose, 2 mM ethylenediaminetetra-
acetic acid (EDTA), and 20 mM Tris–HCl (pH 7.4) at a ratio of 10–
20ll/mg tissue The homogenate was centrifuged at 5500 gfor
3 min, and the supernatant obtained was subsequently centrifugedat 8500 gfor 10 min The pellet was suspended in 1 ml of sucrose
medium and centrifuged again at 8500 gfor 10 min This step
was repeated twice to obtain a mitochondrial enriched fraction
(namely crude mitochondria) suspended in 0.1–0.2 ml of
respiratory medium and used for mitochondrial functional
measurements Analyses of outer and inner mitochondrial membrane integrity
The outer membrane integrity in crude mitochondria isolated
from different cryopreserved brain areas was checked by (i) ascor-
bate (ASC) test [11] and (ii) cytochrome c(cyt c) test [12] The ASC
test was carried out monitoring ASC oxidation, which occurs only
in case of disruption of the outer mitochondrial membrane becauseASC cannot permeate it [13] ASC oxidation was checked polaro-
graphically by measuring oxygen consumption after the addition
of 5 mM ASC to crude brain mitochondria (0.25–0.5 mg protein)
incubated at 37 /C176C in 1 ml of respiratory medium in the presence
of 3
lM ROT, 2 lM AA, and 3 lM myxothiazole (MYXO), followed
by the addition of 0.2 mM N,N,N0,N0-tetramethyl- p-phenylenedi-
amine (TMPD) and 1 mM cyanide (CN/C0) The percentage of integ-
rity of outer membrane was calculated as 1 /C0(vo/C0TMPD/

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Animals were sacrificed and their brains were dissected, either entire hemispheres or isolated brain regions.
2. Brain samples were immediately processed for mitochondria isolation (fresh samples), frozen in dry ice (frozen samples), or cryopreserved.
3. For cryopreservation, brain samples were dissected in an ice-cold solution and transferred into a cryotube containing a solution with glycerol and BSA.
4. Cryopreserved brain tissues were thawed, washed in ice-cold respiratory medium, and transferred to tubes.
5. Brain tissues in tubes were homogenized in sucrose medium at a ratio of 10-20 μL/mg tissue.
6. The homogenate was centrifuged at 5500 g for 3 min, and the supernatant obtained was subsequently centrifuged at 8500 g for 10 min.
7. The pellet was suspended in sucrose medium and centrifuged again at 8500 g for 10 min. This step was repeated twice to obtain a mitochondrial enriched fraction.
8. The mitochondrial enriched fraction was suspended in respiratory medium and used for mitochondrial functional measurements.
9. The outer membrane integrity of crude mitochondria was checked using the ascorbate test and cytochrome c test.
10. The ascorbate test involved monitoring ASC oxidation by measuring oxygen consumption after the addition of ASC to crude brain mitochondria.
11. The cytochrome c test involved monitoring the rate of oxygen consumption under state 3 of respiration after the addition of cytochrome c to isolated mitochondria.
12. The inner membrane integrity was assessed by measuring citrate synthase activity in isolated mitochondria before and after membrane disruption by Triton X-100.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide details on the specific methods used for cryopreservation or mitochondrial functional measurements.
- The specific composition of the sucrose medium and respiratory medium is not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a general outline of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation from mouse brain samples. However, there are gaps in the protocol, particularly regarding cryopreservation and mitochondrial functional measurements. Additional information is needed to fully replicate the protocol.

summary:
Revised Summary: Chemicals of the purest grade, including Rotenone, Antimycin A, Mixothiazol, Oligomycin, and FCCP, were used in Tris salts at pH 7.0–7.4. The animal experimental model consisted of female mice of the outbred CD-1 Swiss-derived strain (ICR) obtained from a commercial breeder (Harlan). The mice were housed in an air-conditioned room (temperature = 21 ± 1°C, relative humidity = 60 ± 10%, lights on from 20.00 to 08.00 h) in polycarbonate transparent cages with a metal top and sawdust bedding. They were provided with a complete pellet diet ad libitum (Altromin, Germany) and tap water. At approximately 4 months of age, the mice were sacrificed, and their brains were dissected. Brain samples were immediately processed for mitochondria isolation (fresh samples), frozen in dry ice (frozen samples), or cryopreserved.

For cryopreservation, brain samples were dissected in an ice-cold solution consisting of 50 mM K-Mes (pH 7.1), 3m MK 2HPO 4, 9.5 mM MgCl 2, and 3 mM ATP. The samples were then transferred into a cryotube containing 500 ll of the same solution plus 20% glycerol and 10 mg/ml fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin (BSA). The samples were frozen in liquid isopentane to achieve rapid and uniform freezing and were maintained at -30 to -35°C. Subsequently, all samples were stored at -80°C or stored in dry ice and shipped.

Mitochondria were isolated from the fresh, frozen, or cryopreserved brain samples using a sucrose medium. The outer mitochondrial membrane integrity was assessed using the ascorbate (ASC) test and cytochrome c (cyt c) test. The inner mitochondrial membrane integrity was evaluated by measuring citrate synthase activity before and after membrane disruption by Triton X-100 detergent.

All procedures were carried out at 4°C to maintain sample integrity. The study focused on the mitochondrial functional measurements and the integrity of the outer and inner mitochondrial membranes in different brain regions of female mice using various experimental techniques and conditions.

Mitochondrial membrane potential ( Dw) generation was mea-
sured by monitoring ﬂuorescence changes of the probe safranin
O at 520-nm excitation and 570-nm emission wavelengths, essen-

results in a decrease of ﬂuorescence Depolarization results in the

rate
The rate of ATP production by isolated mitochondria was deter-

isolated mitochondria from entire hemispheres or isolated brain
regions (0.1–0.3 mg protein) were incubated at 37 /C176C in 2 ml of
respiratory medium in the presence of the ATP detecting system
(ATP ds) consisting of glucose (2.5 mM), hexokinase (HK, 2 e.u.),
glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6P-DH, 1 e.u.), and NADP+
(0.25 mM) plus diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A, 10 lM) in

(5 mM each) or SUCC (5 mM) plus 3 lM ROT (3 lM) as energy
sources The reduction of NADP+in the extramitochondrial phase,
which reveals ATP formation from externally added ADP(0.5 mM), was monitored as an increase in absorbance at
340 nm Care was taken to use enough HK/G6P-DH-coupled en-

measurement of ATP production Statistical analysis
All data are expressed as means ± standard deviations Statisti-
cal evaluation of the differential analysis was performed by Stu-
dent’s ttest The threshold for statistical signiﬁcance was set at
P< 0.05 Results and discussion
Effect of glycerol freeze–thawing on membrane integrity of
mitochondria isolated from cryopreserved mouse brain areas
Measurement of mitochondrial bioenergetics would provide
valuable insight into, and add to the knowledge of, several neuro-
logical disorders and possibly lead to the identiﬁcation of novel
targets for drug discovery However, mitochondrial function anal-
yses such as ATP synthesis and Dwgeneration, the most reliable
means of assaying oxidative phosphorylation defects, should be
performed soon after brain biopsy to guarantee stable mitochon-
dria Although mitochondrial functional integrity has been estab-
lished inside cryopreserved permeabilized muscle ﬁbers [8],a s
well as in cryopreserved isolated rat brain mitochondria [9],t o
date mitochondria isolated from cryopreserved brain tissues have
not been used for studies of energy metabolism Here we carried
out, for the ﬁrst time, bioenergetics measurements using mito-
chondria isolated from cryopreserved mouse brain tissues as com-
pared with those obtained with mitochondria isolated from freshtissues Several additives were screened in other studies as cryoprotec-
tive agents, including sugars, serum proteins, dimethyl sulfoxide
(DMSO), and glycerol [8,16]  Although Kuznetsov and coworkers
[8]showed in muscle ﬁbers a higher cryoprotection potential of
DMSO (20–50% concentration) with respect to glycerol (20% con-
centration) for mitochondrial function measurements, other stud-
ies have shown glycerol to be less toxic than DMSO [16] In good
agreement with the latter, we found 50% rupture of outer mito-
chondrial membrane during freeze–thawing brain tissues in a
medium containing 20–40% DMSO (data not shown), whereas
glycerol (20%), in the presence of 10 mg/ml BSA, was found to be
more effective for cryopreservation of either entire brain hemi-
spheres or dissected brain regions such as cortex, hippocampus,
striatum, and cerebellum, preserving mitochondrial integrity
and functionality, as shown by the measurements reported
below Moreover, the procedure used to isolate mitochondria

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Measurements of citrate synthase activity were carried out at 37°C in 0.8 ml of assay medium consisting of 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 1 mM MgCl2, and 20 mM Hepes/Tris (pH 8.2) in the presence of saturating concentrations of acetyl-coenzyme A (CoA) (0.3 mM), oxaloacetate (0.5 mM), and dithionitrobenzoic acid (DNTB, 0.2 mM), following the increase in absorbance at 412 nm due to thionitrobenzoic acid (TNB) formation.
2. Mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨ) generation was measured by monitoring fluorescence changes of the probe safranin O at 520-nm excitation and 570-nm emission wavelengths. Isolated mouse brain mitochondria (1 mg protein) were incubated at 37°C in 2 ml of medium consisting of 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 1 mM MgCl2, and 20 mM Hepes–Tris (pH 7.0) plus 10 μM safranin O. ΔΨ generation was monitored by adding 5 mM SUCC plus 3 μM ROT or glutamate (GLU) plus malate (MAL) (5 mM each) in the presence of 0.5 mM ADP. ΔΨ generation was abolished by adding the uncoupler FCCP (1.25 μM). Changes of safranin O fluorescence were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS50 spectrofluorimeter.
3. The rate of ATP production by isolated mitochondria was determined by incubating small aliquots of isolated mitochondria (0.1–0.3 mg protein) at 37°C in 2 ml of respiratory medium in the presence of the ATP detecting system (ATP ds). The ATP ds consisted of glucose (2.5 mM), hexokinase (HK, 2 e.u.), glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6P-DH, 1 e.u.), and NADP+ (0.25 mM) plus diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A, 10 μM) in the absence (basal ATP production) or presence of GLU plus MAL (5 mM each) or SUCC (5 mM) plus 3 μM ROT (3 μM) as energy sources. The reduction of NADP+ in the extramitochondrial phase, which reveals ATP formation from externally added ADP (0.5 mM), was monitored as an increase in absorbance at 340 nm.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It only mentions the steps for measuring citrate synthase activity, mitochondrial membrane potential generation, and spectrophotometric determination of mitochondrial ATP production rate. The actual isolation steps are not described.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides steps for measuring citrate synthase activity, mitochondrial membrane potential generation, and spectrophotometric determination of mitochondrial ATP production rate. However, it does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. The actual steps for isolating mitochondria are not described in the article.

summary:
Measurements of citrate synthase activity were carried out at 37°C in a 0.8 ml assay medium. The medium consisted of 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 1 mM MgCl2, and 20 mM Hepes/Tris (pH 8.2), with saturating concentrations of acetyl-coenzyme A (CoA), oxaloacetate, and dithionitrobenzoic acid (DNTB). The absorbance at 412 nm was measured using a Jasco V-550 UV/VIS spectrophotometer to determine thionitrobenzoic acid (TNB) formation.

Mitochondrial membrane potential (Dw) generation was measured by monitoring the fluorescence changes of the probe safranin O at 520 nm excitation and 570 nm emission wavelengths. Safranin O accumulation in mitochondria is driven by membrane potential, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence. Depolarization leads to the release of safranin O and an increase in fluorescence. Isolated mouse brain mitochondria were incubated in a medium consisting of 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM KCl, 1 mM MgCl2, and 20 mM Hepes–Tris (pH 7.0), with safranin O and energy sources. Dw generation was monitored, and changes in safranin O fluorescence were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS50 spectrofluorimeter.

The rate of ATP production by isolated mitochondria was determined using the ATP detecting system (ATP ds). Isolated mitochondria were incubated in respiratory medium with the ATP ds in the absence or presence of energy sources. The reduction of NADP+ in the extramitochondrial phase, indicating ATP formation, was monitored by an increase in absorbance at 340 nm.

Mitochondrial bioenergetics measurements were performed using mitochondria isolated from cryopreserved mouse brain tissues. Additives such as glycerol were used for cryopreservation, and glycerol (20%) in the presence of BSA (10 mg/ml) was found to be effective in preserving mitochondrial integrity and functionality. The procedure used for isolation yielded 1-2 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram of wet brain tissue weight.

article:
Chunk # 3.
Thus, starting from a minimal amount of 0.04 g of brain
tissue (striatum and hippocampus), it is possible to obtain approx-
imately 0.5 mg of mitochondrial protein, sufﬁcient to carry out
measurement of both mitochondrial integrity and ATP production
by oxidative phosphorylation Mitochondrial membrane integrity is an important factor when
assessing the function of isolated mitochondria; thus, we checked
the integrity of mitochondrial outer and inner membranes on
mitochondria isolation from cryopreserved brain tissues as com-
pared with fresh brain tissues To this aim, we checked the outer
mitochondrial membrane integrity by the polarographic cyt ctest
(Fig 1 A) Given cyt cloosely bound to the mitochondrial inner
membrane, when the outer membrane of mitochondria is intact,
the endogenous cyt cstays in the intermembrane space and the
addition of exogenous cyt chas no effect on respiration Con-
versely, when the outer membrane is damaged, the endogenous
cytccan be released from intermembrane space, becoming rate
limiting to the oxygen consumption, and the addition of exogenous
cytcwill markedly stimulate the respiration rate We found that
the rate of oxygen consumption induced by SUCC under state 3
of respiration did not increase after the addition of cyt c, thereby
indicating no outer mitochondrial membrane rupture in mitochon-
drial preparations from cryopreserved mouse brain samples Con-
versely, the addition of cyt cto mitochondria from frozen non-
cryopreserved brain hemisphere increased the rate of oxygen con-
sumption induced by SUCC under state 3 of respiration, indicating
outer mitochondrial membrane damage (see inset of Fig 1 A) The outer mitochondrial membrane integrity was further con-
ﬁrmed by measuring oxygen consumption by mitochondria added
with ASC, which cannot permeate the outer mitochondrial mem-
brane [11]; thus, its oxidation can occur only under conditions of
loss of outer mitochondrial membrane integrity No oxygen con-
sumption was observed after ASC addition to mitochondria ob-
tained from both cryopreserved mouse brain hemisphere and
cortex samples ( Fig 1 B), indicating complete intactness of outer
mitochondrial membrane As expected, strong ASC-dependent
oxygen consumption was observed on the addition of TMPD, an
intermediate electron donor to COX The ﬁnal addition of CN–,
inhibitor of COX, completely prevented oxygen consumption as ex-
pected The integrity of the outer mitochondrial membrane was

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from cryopreserved brain tissues:

1. Start with a minimal amount of 0.04 g of brain tissue (striatum and hippocampus).
2. Obtain approximately 0.5 mg of mitochondrial protein from the brain tissue.
3. Check the integrity of the mitochondrial outer and inner membranes on mitochondria isolation from cryopreserved brain tissues compared to fresh brain tissues.
4. Check the outer mitochondrial membrane integrity using the polarographic cyt ctest.
5. If the outer membrane of mitochondria is intact, the endogenous cyt c stays in the intermembrane space, and the addition of exogenous cyt c has no effect on respiration.
6. If the outer membrane is damaged, the endogenous cyt c can be released from the intermembrane space, becoming rate-limiting to oxygen consumption, and the addition of exogenous cyt c will stimulate the respiration rate.
7. Measure the rate of oxygen consumption induced by SUCC under state 3 of respiration.
8. If the rate of oxygen consumption does not increase after the addition of cyt c, it indicates no outer mitochondrial membrane rupture in mitochondrial preparations from cryopreserved mouse brain samples.
9. If the rate of oxygen consumption increases after the addition of cyt c, it indicates outer mitochondrial membrane damage.
10. Confirm the outer mitochondrial membrane integrity by measuring oxygen consumption by mitochondria added with ASC, which cannot permeate the outer mitochondrial membrane.
11. No oxygen consumption should be observed after ASC addition to mitochondria obtained from cryopreserved mouse brain hemisphere and cortex samples, indicating complete intactness of the outer mitochondrial membrane.
12. Observe ASC-dependent oxygen consumption on the addition of TMPD, an intermediate electron donor to COX.
13. The final addition of CN-, an inhibitor of COX, should completely prevent oxygen consumption.
14. Measure the integrity of the outer mitochondrial membrane in mitochondria isolated from fresh, frozen, and cryopreserved brain hemispheres and from cryopreserved cortex by measuring the rate of ASC-dependent oxygen consumption before and after TMPD addition.
15. Measure the integrity of the inner mitochondrial membrane by assaying the activity of citrate synthase, a marker enzyme of the mitochondrial matrix, before and after membrane disruption by Triton X-100.
16. The inner membrane integrity should be similar in mitochondria isolated from both fresh and cryopreserved brain tissues, but decreased in frozen samples.

Conclusion: The article provides a detailed description of the steps for isolating mitochondria from cryopreserved brain tissues. The protocol includes checking the integrity of the outer and inner mitochondrial membranes and measuring oxygen consumption to assess membrane integrity. However, some steps are not fully described, such as the specific method for mitochondria isolation and the conditions for measuring oxygen consumption.

summary: Starting with a small amount of brain tissue (0.04 g), we can obtain enough mitochondrial protein (0.5 mg) for measuring mitochondrial integrity and ATP production. The integrity of the mitochondrial outer and inner membranes was assessed in cryopreserved brain tissues compared to fresh tissues. The outer membrane integrity was confirmed using a polarographic cyt c test, which showed no rupture in cryopreserved brain samples. The integrity of the outer membrane was further confirmed by measuring oxygen consumption with ASC, which indicated complete intactness. The inner membrane integrity was checked using the activity of citrate synthase, showing similar integrity in fresh and cryopreserved brain tissues. Cryopreservation significantly decreased the integrity of both the outer and inner mitochondrial membranes.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Mitochondria were incubated in the presence of 3 μM ROT, 2 μM AA, and 3 μM MYXO.
2. At the arrows, 5 mM ASC, 0.2 mM TMPD, and 1 mM CN/C0 were added.
3. Ascorbate oxidation (ngat O/min/mg protein) was measured as a marker of the integrity of the mitochondrial outer membrane in crude mitochondria isolated from fresh, frozen, and cryopreserved mouse brains.
4. Hemisphere Cortex Fresh Frozen Cryopreserved Cryopreserved /C0TMPD: 1 ± 1 43 ± 7 ⁄2±1 1±1 +TMPD: 80 ± 3 45 ± 5⁄80 ± 4 64 ± 3
5. The rate of citrate synthase activity (nmol/min/mg protein) was measured in crude mitochondria isolated from fresh, frozen, and cryopreserved mouse brain areas.
6. Hemisphere Cortex Fresh Frozen Cryopreserved Cryopreserved /C0Triton X-100: 90 ± 5 222 ± 4 ⁄110 ± 5 100 ± 7 +Triton X-100: 600 ± 5 230 ± 3⁄580 ± 7 450 ± 8
7. Mitochondria (0.04 mg protein) were incubated at 37°C in 0.8 ml of respiratory medium in the presence of 0.3 mM acetyl-CoA, 0.5 mM oxaloacetate, and 0.2 mM DNTB.
8. No significant differences were found between values measured in mitochondria from cryopreserved and fresh hemispheres (P > 0.05).
9. Significant differences were found between frozen and cryopreserved samples (P < 0.05).
10. Active mitochondria from cryopreserved brain areas did not significantly differ from those obtained from fresh brain areas.
11. The complex I inhibitor ROT (3 μM) and the complex III inhibitor AA (2 μM) prevented GLU plus MAL- and SUCC-dependent generation, respectively.
12. The addition of the uncoupler FCCP abolished generation in both cryopreserved and fresh brain samples.
13. The rate of ATP synthesis by mitochondria was measured by measuring the rate of ATP appearance in the extramitochondrial phase due to ADP addition.
14. ATP production was measured in fresh or cryopreserved mouse brain mitochondria in the absence or presence of the respiratory substrate SUCC or GLU plus MAL.
15. Generation A and B were shown in Fig. 2, indicating mitochondrial membrane potential generation in isolated mitochondria from cryopreserved and fresh mouse brain hemispheres.
16. The rate of generation is expressed as DF520/570 /min mg mitochondrial protein.
17. Data are reported as means ± standard deviations of measurements on three experiments carried out using different mitochondrial preparations.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps for mitochondrial isolation, including incubation conditions, addition of specific substances, measurement of outer and inner membrane integrity, and assessment of ATP synthesis and membrane potential generation. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, as it lacks information on the initial isolation procedure and specific details of the incubation conditions.

summary: Mitochondria were incubated with ROT, AA, and MYXO. ASC, TMPD, and CN/C0 were added later. Oxygen consumption rates were measured. Outer membrane integrity was assessed using ascorbate oxidation assay in crude mitochondria isolated from fresh, frozen, and cryopreserved mouse brains. Inner membrane integrity was measured using citrate synthase activity assay. No significant differences were found between cryopreserved and fresh hemispheres. ATP synthesis rate was measured to evaluate the efficiency of oxidative phosphorylation in cryopreserved brain mitochondria. Mitochondrial membrane potential generation was observed in isolated mitochondria from cryopreserved and fresh mouse brain hemispheres.

ing procedure of mitochondria isolation allow obtaining a good

ies of mitochondrial bioenergetics Conclusion
We have presented a procedure of brain cryopreservation and
isolation of mitochondria ensuring their integrity and maintenance
of optimal efﬁciency of mitochondrial ATP production and mem-
brane potential generation This procedure could be suitable for
bioenergetics analysis of mitochondrial physiology and screening
of mitochondrial injuries in various neurological diseases The very small amounts of sample used in this procedure allow
carrying out studies of oxidative phosphorylation also in biological
samples where the amount of available tissue is limited such as
striatum and hippocampus whose weight is approximately 0.04 g
in mouse Other procedures require greater amounts of starting
material because they make use of centrifugation in density gradi-
ent to purify the mitochondrial fraction (for references, see Ref.[17]) and, therefore, cannot be used for biopsy specimens Our approach, therefore, can be used as an attractive alternative
to the investigation of bioenergetics requiring mitochondria isola-
tion immediately after explants of brains and/or of brain areas; in-
stead, brain samples, immediately cryopreserved and batch stored,
can be used successively for bioenergetic analyses

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Mitochondria were isolated from cryopreserved and fresh mouse brain hemispheres.
2. The brain tissue was homogenized in a respiration medium.
3. The homogenate was centrifuged to obtain a mitochondrial pellet.
4. The mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in a respiration medium.
5. The resuspended mitochondria were incubated at 37°C.
6. Different respiratory substrates were added to the respiration medium: GLU plus MAL (5 mM each) or SUCC (5 mM) plus 3 μM rotenone.
7. ATP-ds plus 10 μM Ap5A was added to the respiration medium.
8. ADP (0.5 mM) was added to the respiration medium in some experiments.
9. The increase in absorbance at 340 nm was measured as tangents to the initial slopes.
10. ATP production rates were measured and expressed as nmol NADP+ reduced/min/mg mitochondrial protein.

**Incompleteness:**

- The article does not provide detailed information on the specific steps involved in homogenization and centrifugation to obtain the mitochondrial pellet.
- The article does not describe the specific composition of the respiration medium used.
- The article does not provide information on the duration of the incubation period.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a general overview of the mitochondrial isolation procedure, including the steps involved in obtaining the mitochondrial pellet and measuring ATP production rates. However, there are gaps in the protocol, particularly regarding the details of homogenization, centrifugation, and the composition of the respiration medium. Additional information would be needed to replicate the protocol accurately.

summary: The rate of ATP production by cryo-preserved mouse brain mitochondria significantly increased compared to the basal rate when SUCC or GLU plus MAL was present. Mitochondria isolated from fresh brain tissue showed the same increase in ATP production. Cryopreserved mitochondria from different brain areas did not differ significantly from fresh mitochondria in terms of ATP production rate. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the cryopreservation method in maintaining mitochondrial efficiency. The procedure allows for the isolation of intact and functional mitochondria suitable for studying mitochondrial bioenergetics in various neurological diseases. The small sample size required makes it suitable for limited tissue availability. This approach provides an alternative to other methods that require larger amounts of starting material and cannot be used for biopsy specimens.

summary: Revised Summary: Cryopreservation of brain tissues enables the integration of various approaches in mitochondrial research by sharing samples among scientific centers. It ensures long-term stability without affecting potential mitochondrial defects, allowing investigations into bioenergetics in neurological diseases. This study is partially supported by grants from the Italian Ministry of Instruction, the University and Research (MIUR), and the Fondation Jerome Lejeune. The researchers also collaborate with Annarita Armenise for technical assistance. The FIRB-MERIT program focuses on MECP2 phosphorylation in Rett syndrome. Scientists like L. Galluzzi, J. Nunnari, and K. L. Wicks study mitochondrial function and cryopreservation methods in animal models and human biopsies. They investigate the impact of mitochondrial impairment on bioenergetics and oxidative damage in the prefrontal cortex. The IRE-IFO has also contributed to the study of MECP2 phosphorylation. Cryopreservation methods and cryoprotectants have been compared for fowl spermatozoa and CNS mitochondria isolation. Active mitochondria have been successfully isolated from cryopreserved brain areas.

What are the potential mitochondrial defects that can be investigated in neurological diseases using cryopreserved brain tissues?
